content.text: Wrapping a Claude API service in a container is straightforward — the service is a stateless HTTP gateway that calls an external API, requiring no GPU, no large model weights, and no special privileges. The challenge is production hardening: safe secret injection, correct resource limits, graceful shutdown, and health probes that accurately reflect the service's readiness to handle requests.
What it is

Containerising a Claude API service means packaging the application code, runtime, and configuration into a Docker image that runs identically across development, staging, and production environments. Because the service calls the Anthropic API rather than running a local model, the image is small (typically 100–400 MB) and requires only CPU resources. The container's only privileged dependency is the ANTHROPIC_API_KEY, which is injected at runtime from a secrets manager rather than baked into the image.

In Kubernetes, the containerised service is deployed as a Deployment with multiple replicas behind a Service, enabling horizontal scaling and rolling updates. A HorizontalPodAutoscaler (HPA) adjusts replica count based on CPU utilization — the metric that best tracks request load for a CPU-bound HTTP proxy. Readiness and liveness probes ensure that traffic is routed only to healthy pods and that crashed pods are restarted automatically.
Why it matters

Containerisation enforces environment parity. A service that works on a developer's laptop with a locally exported API key often fails in CI or production because of dependency version drift or implicit environment assumptions. A well-built Docker image eliminates these differences, making deployments predictable and rollbacks safe.

Security is also a primary motivation. Hard-coding API keys in environment files checked into git, or passing them as plaintext environment variables in Kubernetes manifests, is a common source of credential leaks. The patterns described here — Kubernetes Secrets, external secret stores, and non-root containers — address the most common misconfiguration vectors.
Benefits
Environment parity across dev, staging, and production — the same image runs everywhere.
Small image size — no model weights, only Python/Node runtime and application code.
Horizontal scaling via Kubernetes HPA with minimal configuration.
Rolling updates with zero downtime using RollingUpdate deployment strategy.
Secret isolation — API keys injected at runtime, never baked into images.
Trade-offs

Container cold-start latency is real but manageable. Python container startup (importing anthropic, FastAPI, and related libraries) takes 1–4 seconds. For latency-sensitive endpoints, keep a minimum of 2 replicas running at all times to avoid cold-start latency on the first request after a scale-down. Kubernetes minReplicas in the HPA controls this.

Network egress to api.anthropic.com adds a policy consideration. In locked-down Kubernetes clusters with default-deny network policies, the Claude service pod must be explicitly allowed outbound HTTPS to api.anthropic.com on port 443. Missing this allowance is one of the most common deployment failures.
How it works

The container follows a four-layer build: base image (Python slim or distroless), dependency installation (pip install), application copy, and entrypoint definition. Multi-stage builds keep the final image small by separating the build environment (which may include compilers for C extensions) from the runtime environment. The ANTHROPIC_API_KEY is not present in the image; it is injected as an environment variable from a Kubernetes Secret at pod creation time.
Architecture

attrs.alt: Containerised Claude API service Kubernetes architecture
attrs.title: Claude Service — Kubernetes Architecture

content.text: Traffic flows from the internet through an Ingress controller (NGINX or Traefik) to a Kubernetes Service (ClusterIP load balancer) and then to one of several Claude service pods. Each pod reads ANTHROPIC_API_KEY from a Kubernetes Secret at startup. The HPA monitors CPU utilization and adjusts replica count between a configured minimum and maximum. Pods make outbound HTTPS calls to api.anthropic.com.
When to use it

Use full Kubernetes deployment for production services handling sustained traffic (more than a few requests per minute) or when your organization already operates a Kubernetes cluster. For development, testing, and low-traffic internal tools, docker-compose or a single container on a VM is simpler and sufficient.
How to implement
Dockerfile

# syntax=docker/dockerfile:1

# Stage 1: dependency builder
FROM python:3.12-slim AS builder
WORKDIR /build
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install --prefix=/install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt

# Stage 2: runtime image
FROM python:3.12-slim AS runtime

# Non-root user for security
RUN addgroup --system appgroup && adduser --system --ingroup appgroup appuser
USER appuser
WORKDIR /app

# Copy installed packages from builder
COPY --from=builder /install /usr/local

# Copy application code
COPY --chown=appuser:appgroup . .

# Health check (optional — Kubernetes probes are preferred)
HEALTHCHECK --interval=30s --timeout=5s --retries=3 CMD python -c "import httpx; httpx.get('http://localhost:8080/health').raise_for_status()"

ENV PORT=8080
EXPOSE 8080

# Graceful shutdown: uvicorn handles SIGTERM
CMD ["uvicorn", "main:app", "--host", "0.0.0.0", "--port", "8080", "--workers", "2"]
Application (FastAPI)
Kubernetes manifests

# secret.yaml (apply first; use ExternalSecrets or Vault in production)
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: claude-credentials
type: Opaque
stringData:
  ANTHROPIC_API_KEY: "sk-ant-..."   # replace at deploy time
---
# deployment.yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: claude-service
spec:
  replicas: 2
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: claude-service
  strategy:
    type: RollingUpdate
    rollingUpdate:
      maxSurge: 1
      maxUnavailable: 0   # zero-downtime rollout
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: claude-service
    spec:
      terminationGracePeriodSeconds: 30
      containers:
        - name: claude-service
          image: your-registry/claude-service:latest
          ports:
            - containerPort: 8080
          env:
            - name: ANTHROPIC_API_KEY
              valueFrom:
                secretKeyRef:
                  name: claude-credentials
                  key: ANTHROPIC_API_KEY
          resources:
            requests:
              cpu: "250m"
              memory: "256Mi"
            limits:
              cpu: "1000m"
              memory: "1Gi"
          readinessProbe:
            httpGet:
              path: /health
              port: 8080
            initialDelaySeconds: 5
            periodSeconds: 10
          livenessProbe:
            httpGet:
              path: /health
              port: 8080
            initialDelaySeconds: 10
            periodSeconds: 30
          securityContext:
            runAsNonRoot: true
            allowPrivilegeEscalation: false
            readOnlyRootFilesystem: true
---
# hpa.yaml
apiVersion: autoscaling/v2
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
metadata:
  name: claude-service-hpa
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: claude-service
  minReplicas: 2
  maxReplicas: 10
  metrics:
    - type: Resource
      resource:
        name: cpu
        target:
          type: Utilization
          averageUtilization: 60
Real-world example

A customer support platform serving 50,000 daily active users runs a Claude service on Kubernetes with 2 baseline replicas and HPA scaling to 12 at peak. Each pod handles ~150 concurrent slow-streaming Claude requests. With maxUnavailable: 0 and maxSurge: 1 in the rolling update strategy, model updates deploy in under 5 minutes with zero user-visible downtime. The ANTHROPIC_API_KEY is managed by External Secrets Operator pulling from AWS Secrets Manager, refreshed hourly without pod restarts.
Common mistakes

Running containers as root is the most common security mistake. Add USER nobody (or a named non-root user) to the Dockerfile and set runAsNonRoot: true in the pod security context. Also set readOnlyRootFilesystem: true unless the application writes to disk — Claude API services typically do not.

# WRONG: default root user
FROM python:3.12-slim
WORKDIR /app
COPY . .
CMD ["uvicorn", "main:app", "--host", "0.0.0.0", "--port", "8080"]

# RIGHT: explicit non-root user
FROM python:3.12-slim
RUN adduser --system appuser
USER appuser
WORKDIR /app
COPY --chown=appuser . .
CMD ["uvicorn", "main:app", "--host", "0.0.0.0", "--port", "8080"]

Missing resource requests causes Kubernetes to schedule too many pods on a single node, leading to CPU throttling and Out-Of-Memory kills. Always specify both requests and limits. For Claude API services, cpu request of 250m and memory request of 256Mi are reasonable starting points.
Best practices
Use multi-stage builds — separate the dependency-installation stage from the runtime stage to minimize image size and surface area.
Pin base image versions — FROM python:3.12.9-slim-bookworm, not FROM python:3-slim, to prevent unexpected breaking changes.
Set terminationGracePeriodSeconds to at least 30 seconds so in-flight Claude streaming responses complete before the pod is killed.
Never put ANTHROPIC_API_KEY in the Dockerfile or docker-compose.yml checked into git — use environment variable injection at runtime.
Configure an explicit network policy allowing outbound HTTPS to api.anthropic.com if your cluster uses default-deny egress rules.
Performance & scalability

Claude API services are I/O-bound, not CPU-bound. Each request spends most of its time waiting for the Anthropic API to return tokens. A single uvicorn worker with asyncio can handle 50–200 concurrent slow-streaming requests. Set --workers to match the number of CPU cores for CPU-bound preprocessing (embedding, chunking) but keep it at 1–2 for pure proxy services to avoid excessive memory use.

Connection pooling to the Anthropic API is handled automatically by the httpx client inside the Anthropic SDK. The default pool size of 100 connections per client is sufficient for most workloads. For very high-throughput deployments (thousands of simultaneous requests), initialize multiple Anthropic client instances and round-robin across them.
Tooling & ecosystem

Helm charts are the standard distribution mechanism for multi-manifest Kubernetes deployments. Package the deployment, service, HPA, and secret reference into a Helm chart with configurable values (image tag, replica count, resource limits) for environment-specific overrides. Tools like Kustomize provide similar parameterization without the template language.

For secret management beyond Kubernetes Secrets, the External Secrets Operator integrates with AWS Secrets Manager, GCP Secret Manager, HashiCorp Vault, and Azure Key Vault. It creates Kubernetes Secrets from external stores and refreshes them automatically, eliminating manual secret rotation steps.
Comparison with alternatives
Deployment
Scaling
Ops complexity
Best for
Kubernetes (this)
HPA auto-scaling
High
Production, high traffic
docker-compose
Manual
Low
Dev, internal tools
AWS Lambda / Cloud Run
Auto, per-request
Low
Bursty / low-volume traffic
Managed (Modal, Fly.io)
Auto, per-container
Medium
Quick deploy, no k8s ops
Security & reliability

The ANTHROPIC_API_KEY must never appear in image layers, Kubernetes manifests committed to git, or application logs. Use Kubernetes Secrets for basic deployments and External Secrets Operator backed by a secret manager (AWS Secrets Manager, Vault) for production. Enable secret rotation by updating the secret store entry and rolling the Kubernetes Deployment — pods pick up the new key on restart.

Set readOnlyRootFilesystem: true and allowPrivilegeEscalation: false in the pod security context. Add a NetworkPolicy that restricts outbound traffic to only api.anthropic.com:443 and the cluster DNS. These two controls eliminate the most common container escape and lateral movement vectors.
Key takeaways
Claude API services are stateless, CPU-bound containers with no GPU requirement and small image sizes (100–400 MB).
Inject ANTHROPIC_API_KEY from a Kubernetes Secret or external secret store — never bake it into the image.
Run as a non-root user with readOnlyRootFilesystem: true and allowPrivilegeEscalation: false.
Use RollingUpdate with maxUnavailable: 0 for zero-downtime deployments and set terminationGracePeriodSeconds ≥ 30 for graceful streaming shutdown.
HPA on CPU utilization (60% target) with minReplicas: 2 prevents cold-start latency under normal load.
If the cluster uses default-deny egress NetworkPolicy, add an explicit allow rule for outbound HTTPS to api.anthropic.com:443.
